description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 1, part 4: Translunar Injection & Docking

tokens: One second before ignition, the light for the number one engine in the cluster of five indicator lights comes on.
Actual S-IVB ignition is at 002:33:27.6 GET.
Two seconds later the light goes out.
When it next comes on, it will be to announce J-2 engine cut-off and the end of the burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: One minute, everything looks good on board.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's starting to pick up the yaw, just as programmed.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: What a way to watch a sunrise!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Tom Stafford reporting ¾ of one G.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Coming up 3 minutes; trajectory looks great.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"The S-IVB lit off exactly to the second, on time.
We were all getting very sensitive to any motion.
The thing we noticed right away was the growling of the S-IVB and these oscillations.
There were little lateral and longitudinal oscillations and a growl.
Then between 3 minutes and 3 minutes and 5 seconds, a high-frequency oscillation noise and vibration were superimposed upon the growling.
All three of us thought the flight was going to be over right then."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"It was a zzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzz - like that.
Maybe 60 cycles or somewhere around there.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"And you could feel the vibration in the couches."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"Feel it and hear it.
So we all figured the flight was over right there.
So, from 3 minutes on, we held our breath."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan, from the1969 Technical debrief:"It provoked comments like, 'burn, baby, burn.' You can't call it pogo."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"But we never had a longitudinal pogo.
What we had were these motions."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan, from the1969 Technical debrief:"It was a random buzz."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"This might have been a high-frequency pogo for all we know."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"But it just came on like that, and it lasted all the way through until shutdown."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"We'd never seen it before and never heard about it.
It really scared the hell out of us - not from a safety point of view we thought the flight was going to be over shortly."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan, from the1969 Technical debrief:"And I was trying to figure how we'd do a TLI - plus 10 "
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've got a predicted cut-off: 2 plus 39 plus 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: At 4 minutes everybody is saying Go.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're getting a little [garble] high-frequency vibrations in the cabin.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: At 5 minutes, we still have you Go, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: At cut-off, Up Telemetry, IU to Accept.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The Number 1 light on the launch vehicle indicator comes back on to indicate engine cut-off.

It also signals the beginning of Time-Base 7, concerned with controlling the pressure in the S-IVB fuel tank, and with control of the Saturn's attitude in preparation for the separation and docking manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And would you believe, my Delta-VC.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Their jubilation comes from having seen how accurately their systems can both control and monitor their engine burns.
During the TLI burn, the Saturn's guidance system had to add 10,437.6 feet per second to their velocity.
At the start of the burn, the EMS had the same figure on its display and, using its own accelerometer, was decrementing that number down towards zero as they gained speed.
When the Saturn shut the J-2 engine down, the EMS had overshot zero by only 0.6 fps, meaning that the two independent systems differed by only 0.006 per cent, an astounding accuracy.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Charlie, we've got an O2Flow High and a light in the middle of the burn, here, which we can't account for.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Communications are now via the tracking ship, USNS Redstone.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: In trouble-shooting that thing, I went to Auto 2 on the suit circuit water accumulator.
That's the only thing I could think of.
It was right at 10 minutes when it happened.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We think that cabin pressure regs kicked in for that O2flow, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: They just went out, and the flow is starting to drop now, Charlie.
Looks like we're in good shape.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We think we'll be losing you through the Redstone here in about 30 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: 2 hours and 41 minutes into the mission.
Apollo 10 on the way to the Moon.
We have lost signal for about 3 or 4 minutes.
We will pick up at Hawaii at 2 hours, 44 minutes.
This is Mission Control in Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 2 hours, 44 minutes.
Charlie Duke's just put in a call to the spacecraft.
We're showing present altitude 608 nautical miles.
The velocity starting to drop off already; 33,602 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Would you believe the world is starting to fade away?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: FIDO confirms it was a perfect insertion.
We'd like you to confirm that you're on omni Delta, and we're all Go.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're on omni Delta, Charlie, and there sure ain't any question about it from here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is confirming which of the four S-band antennae mounted around the periphery of the CM is currently selected.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I don't meant the Delta, I mean the world.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We expect the S-IVB to start to Sep attitude at 2 plus 54 plus 10, right on time.
And your Sep attitude is looking good.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The S-IVB maneuvers the whole remaining stack to the separation attitude using the two APS (Auxiliary Propulsion System) units.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: John's in the left seat now, and I'm in the center seat.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Did you get a chance to get that radiation survey meter out?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes, I did, Charlie, and I read zero on every scale.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And what was the GET of that, Gene?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll have you AOS now until you get to LOS at the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Sounds like Chris has pretty good coverage.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is referring to Chris Kraft jnr who, at the time of Apollo 10, was Director of Flight Operations.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 10 was launched to the east and travelled around Earth in that direction.

Now, as it leaves for the Moon, its groundtrack slows and will eventually loop back on itself as Earth's rotation catches up with a spacecraft that is travelling away from the planet rather than across it.
An important effect is that only three major ground stations spaced roughly 120° around the planet are required to give constant communications to the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Chris says there ain't no backing out now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You can play our favorite song, Charlie, the one about Fly me Someplace or Somewhere.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got three Marshall guys here, smiling from ear to ear, too.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The Marshall guys Charlie Duke refers to are the booster systems engineers on the console and what they refer to is the trench.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Velocity is 32,603 feet per second, altitude 857 nautical miles.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We'd like you to do a Verb 66 to get the state vector in the right position.
And, if you've got time, comment on the ORDEAL tracking there for that backup TLI.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The initial track was just right on what the nominal was, and the ORDEAL was looking just what it should be.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"TLI monitor procedure - the new procedure that we worked out with respect to manual backup guidance worked out very well.
We set the Orb-rate ball to the lunar torquing rate at 200 nautical miles - the way it should match coming down - and it worked great.
We were all confident that - if it were required to do a manual TLI, we could have done it - just no problem.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Heart rates during that long S-IVB burn: Tom Stafford, 116; John Young, 97; Gene Cernan, 104.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Looks like we might have a little closed-circuit TV up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is advising that he has the onboard color TV operating and that he has a picture on his monitor.
The picture has not yet been received at MCC Houston.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, I can't really tell too much, but at least it works closed-circuit.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I've got my own little show of these two guys up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I hope it's that good down here.
We're sitting on pins and needles waiting for it.
And, if you've got a second, we'd like to, Tom, we'd like you to recap these S-IVB oscillations; give the guys one up on starting work on it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: All the way through even into Earth orbit boost the S-IVB had just a slight little - it felt like - like both lateral and longitudinal vibrations to it, and after - It felt like it was running rough, at least compared to the Titan.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And then after 3 minutes, superimposed upon the low frequency vibrations, came a real high frequency vib; I'd say in the ball park of 20 cps, something like that.

And of course we were sweating it all the way, but it shut down right on time; but there was a definite shift to a high frequency superimposed upon the low frequency, at about 3 minutes into the TLI burn.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is referring back to the vibrations encountered during the TLI burn.
See crew debrief comments detailed atGET 2 hours, 36 minutes, 30 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Is it a - give you a feeling like it - 2.2 Mach in the 104, something like that, just a real rumble?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We are starting the maneuver now, so we'll tell you later.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: S-IVB is maneuvering to separation attitude.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Altitude is 1,598 miles, velocity 30,077 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We show ourselves at the attitude.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're going to check the thrusters here, now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The crew are checking the operation of the RCS thrusters on the SM, required to control the CSM during the transposition and docking manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 10, you're Go for Pyro Arm and Go for Sep.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Crew has been given the Go to arm the pyrotechnic devices that will separate the CSM from the SLA, and then separate the four SLA panel that have been protecting the stowed LM up until this point.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: He will do the docking to the LM.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: He is now in the left seat flying the Command/Service Module.
A minute and a half from separation.
The Command and Service Modules will separate from the S-IVB at about...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Could you confirm the thruster firings?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We confirm all the rotational inputs; we did not confirm the translational.
As far as we're concerned, you are Go.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: 10, we did see plus and minus X on the translational.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The Noun 22 looks good to us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"We applied 0.6 ft/sec velocity to about 40 seconds and nulled the velocity to zero, essentially.
We then started our pitch around at about a degree and a half per second.
When we got around, we were about 150 feet away from the S-IVB (which is not a bad place to be).
But that's about 100 feet further away than we should have been.
I don't have any explanation for this.
It took us a little more gas to get back there."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young (continued):"Better to be safe than sorry.
We were still moving away from the vehicle when we turned around.
It took three different positive translations of 0.2 to 0.3 ft/sec to start closing on the vehicle."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young (continued):"I don't understand it, but it didn't cause any problem.
If you are going to be safe about it, why not do it that way?"
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young (continued):"We didn't do any formation flying; we just turned around and went back into docking.
Transposition was a degree and a quarter per second to turnaround.
We were doing 0.2 ft/sec and we docked in CMC Auto.
Alignment, as far as I was concerned, was absolutely perfect - a piece of cake."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The handling characteristics are just exactly what they were in the simulator.
It's easy to fly in Auto control.
I don't feel that the sunlight will ever be a problem on docking.
I don't think there is any need to constrain the angle of sunlight except that you'd like to have it somewhere behind you, maybe within plus or minus 60 degrees of being behind you.
It doesn't seem to make any difference.
Now, when we were far out there, coming in, because the brightness off the LM, the COAS was washed out.
But, when we got in closer, it came back in again and was there the whole way in.
As soon as we hit, if I'd fired the bottle we'd have been all right.
But I turned everything off and we sat there, and slowly the vehicle drifted down a couple of degrees.
I wanted to get it back.
So I fired a couple of down pulses and it slowly picked the vehicle right back up and then we fired the bottles and went on in.
I don't really believe we had any misalignment to speak of at contact."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: From theApollo 10 Mission Report:"Separation of the Command and Service Modules from the S-IVB was initiated at 3:02:42 and docking was completed at 3:17:37, but the estimated distance at turnaround was reported to have been 150 feet, instead of the intended 50 feet.

Crew procedures for this maneuver were based on those for Apollo 9 and were executed properly; however, a reduced S-IVB weight from Apollo 9 and the fact that some plus-X translation velocity remained when an attempt was made to null the separation rate probably resulted in the increased separation distance.

The lower S-IVB weight affected separation in that the impulse derived during firing of the pyrotechnic separation charge and the velocity gained from any reaction control plume impingement would both be greater than expected.
Each of these effects have been analyzed, and results show the increased separation distance can be accounted for within the estimation accuracy of the crew."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 10 is pitching around now, the Guidance Control officers says, pitching around and then will come back in and dock with the Lunar Module.
Altitude now 3,580 nautical miles; velocity is down to 25,401.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: How do you read me, Gene?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We don't have the S-IVB yet, but there goes a panel.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan has seen one of the SLA panels separating from the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Holy Moly, I sure hope we can show it to you, I really do.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Charlie Duke is making the observation that he hopes all of the SLA panels are travelling retrograde, that is moving outward and behind the S-IVB / LM combination, so that they do not pose any risk when the CSM moves back in for the docking with the LM 'Snoopy'.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I don't know what [garble] it is up here right now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Those are the SLA panels that house the Lunar Module.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Charlie, I've got the world on closed circuit here, so we're going to try and get High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I should be coming down to you, and I'll have to adjust it as we come along into the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan has now switched from the S-band omnidirectional antenna to the S-band high-gain directional dish antenna (panel 3).
The High Gain Antenna deploys from its stowed position when the CSM separates from the SLA panels.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We got the black and white now with a little time delay on the color.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And the S-IVB ought to be coming in here in a second.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, we got the S-IVB coming into the top.
The sun's really shining on it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll try to adjust it for you
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, we got the color now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'm sorry it's tilted a little bit.
That's the best I could do with the brackets.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: No sweat; we got it right in the center of the screen, Gene.
It looks like the Sun's really bright on it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The Sun's got the S-IVB - the LM sort of blotted out; it's so bright.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, your zooming in looks really good, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, I've got it closed down all the way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The - In the center of the LM now, we still got a real - couple of real bright spots, but it's looking real good in color.
We can, see the probe - correction, the drogue.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The - It's the silver panels that are reflecting back real brightly.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You're drifting off just to the right a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You can't believe the picture we're getting.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll tell you, this monitor makes it great.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You've got it framed just perfectly.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, I think the color will be beautiful once, we can show you the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Old Charlie Brown is a mass of cord and wire floating around here, though.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I've settled down now on the zoom, and the closing rate you see is the closing rate we've got.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene, if it looks like to you - We have a bright spot - it may be on your vidicon, coming in on your vidicon tube on the black and white.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes, we've got it in real life.
The camera is fully in the shade.
That's just a reflection coming right off the - right off of Snoopy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 10, we're afraid you might be burning a hole into your vidicon tube.
Move it off to the - a little bit off of Snoop.
I think those panels are so bright we might be getting problems with the vidicon tube.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I can just cover it up for a while if you like.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'd like the LM - Snoopy back, if you could give it to us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, that's looking great now, except for a couple of fingers there or something.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Good resolution; that's what they were.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You got your big hands in the way.
transcript.speaker: Cooper

tokens: Hey, I don't know what you did, but the - It's really beautiful now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Boy, the color is great, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Cooper

tokens: Hell, I got to watch it on TV, too.
I've got - can't see out the window.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, what's that guy doing on the front porch?
transcript.speaker: Cooper

tokens: Well, all I can say is it's really happening, and what hasn't happened you haven't seen yet.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: He and Charlie Duke are both on the CapCom console.
And that orange platform is the front porch.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Charlie, we can't be more than about 5, 10 feet away.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 10, it's looking real stable to us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Be docked in a second, I hope.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We can see the markings on the rendezvous window.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got a capture; we haven't fired yet.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Gene, we can read the numbers on the LM docking window.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"John gave me the direction to fire the retract bottles, and I threw the switch: The one thing that amazed me, I thought it took a long period of time for the retract cycle to pull in."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford (continued):"I said to myself, 'Is it retracting?' I couldn't see anything.
I was waiting for the barber poles to go gray and hear the rings lock together.
I just wasn't aware of this total time that is required for the retract cycle.
When the docking latch is made, there was a kind of ripple type of situation, a real rapid ripple."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We didn't get any Master Alarm.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Didn't look like there was any hardly any after dock - post docking oscillations.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: 10, that's a great picture of the quads.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll try and take you on a quick tour.
We're - I may have to hold you up for a little bit here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: John's going down to the LEB [Lower Equipment Bay] and, I'm going to the left seat now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'd like you to Safe the Logic.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: See switch diagram atGET 001:51:48when the Pyro Logic was armed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That was one of the Reaction Control System quads you saw there at the last.
We are coming back up here now with another picture.
Altitude 6,869 nautical miles, velocity 21,280 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Charlie, you're going to have to look at the same picture for a while until we get this integrity check complete.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are in the process of attempting to pressurize the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're going to go ahead and suspend the TV here for about 10 minutes until we get a little bit squared away.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll be back with you shortly.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Give me a call when you've got time to copy an evasive PAD.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This PAD will contain the details of the short separation burn which will clear the Apollo 10 spacecraft from the now spent S-IVB third stage.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
